story:
Destiny jingled the moment my human lifted my leash. I sprang from my plush donut throne and galloped to her side. Outside, every scent was a secret, every patch of grass a crime scene. I pulled. She wobbled. I called it teamwork.

Then a blur—threat or snack? I charged. It was a leaf. An aggressive one.

I sat in protest mid-footpath until we turned home. Triumph tastes like nap.

The next day I tested the power of sound. I whined. My humans delivered treats, warmth, and adoration. Royalty confirmed. But caution: too much drama and I risk the Place of Betrayal.

The vet.

I pivoted to strategic sighs and leaned into ear scratches. They called me needy. I called it mastery.

At the faint creak of the pantry, my legs became lightning. A treat appeared; I vanished to my private nibbling corner. Solitary snacking is art. Flair, not drama.

Then came betrayal cloaked in butter-sweet scent. I sat. I stared. I pawed.

No share.

“You can’t have chocolate,” she said, like that helps. Forbidden fruit, indeed. I forgave her eventually. I am gracious.

Rain arrived like a tragedy. My paws are precious, I held it. She brought the towel. I negotiated. We tiptoed the world, and afterward she dried each paw like gemstones. High-maintenance? No. Toasty.

I sit like a gentleman: legs out, back straight, majestic. Once, my human found a lump and panicked. Vet. Diagnosis: I sit weird; the “lump” is just muscle and skin shifting because of my postural flair. The vet gave me treats. I forgave the slander.

Blanket day meant luxury arranged by my assistant (human). She fluffed too slowly, so I helped. She said no. I insisted. Eventually I napped in a half-fluffed masterpiece. A win is a win.

I am not allowed to dig, so I invented the nose-nudge method. Loopholes are legal. Bones must be stored. You never know when a human might crave dirt-seasoned treasures. I buried one with flourish. She watched. I winked.

I’m barred from the bed, but the lounge allows occasional cuddles. I wiggle, squirm, lick, and attempt tasteful claw placements. She says no and laughs. Ten years and counting—I’ll train her yet. She may be bossy, but she is the greatest.

A sock fell. I acquired it. She chased. I hid. She traded treat for sock. I accepted the treat, then reclaimed the sock and sprinted. She laughed. I wagged. Our teamwork shines.

A mirror showed another dog. He barked my bark and wagged my wag.

“It’s you,” she said.

Nonsense. I am handsome, sophisticated; he was a copy. Still, I liked him. 

Vacuum day threatened my nap. I lay like a boulder. She vacuumed around me. I attacked the beast until peace returned. Nap victory secured.

At night, I curled beside her. “You’re my best friend,” she whispered. I pressed closer, licked once (maybe twice), and stayed.

I can be dramatic, selective, and proudly odd. But I love my humans fiercely. And they love me back. Always.
